title: Named for My Grandmother
summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at the Northborough, Massachusetts Free Library in August 2013, Virginia Simms George explains that she was named for her grandmother, not the state, was born in Newport News on the tidewater, has a brother thirteen months older, and was raised mostly by her father after her parents separated when she was young.

title: They Were Such Good People
summary: George recounts moving to the suburbs as white families fled, a departing neighbor telling her father she would never have sold had she known they were good people, a bus ride at about twelve when a white woman recoiled from sitting beside her, and her brother back from Germany rebuking a hardware clerk who called their father a boy.

title: No One Can Take It From You
summary: She tells how the words boy and girl were used in Virginia to demean Black people, how she resolved to become a lawyer and fight for civil rights and majored in history at Virginia Union, and how her postman father, active in organizations, pushed education as the thing no one could take from you.

title: Our Neighborhoods, Our Schools
summary: George describes the totally separated world of segregated Newport News, watching the Brown decision and Little Rock on television, Black neighborhoods, restaurants and stores, and all-Black schools where the principal, counselors and teachers were Black, while her postman father worked a white route and came home at Christmas with bags of presents.

title: Where the Ships Came In
summary: She explains Newport News expanding toward Warwick as white residents left, that African Americans there could register and vote, and the shipyard and military influx that filled the town with sailors at the first of the month, so that she could not go out except chaperoned by her brother or a neighbor.

title: A Debutante in a Cadillac
summary: George recalls the self-contained Black community with its own funeral homes, churches and drugstores, working as a soda jerk in a drugstore owned by Dr. Jones and Dr. Tucker, and being presented as a debutante at sixteen in white dress, gloves and pearls, riding to the event in her uncle's Cadillac in a dress that filled the back seat.

title: Attorney Marion Poe
summary: She names the people who supported her, a friend named Mary whose husband was Virginia's first Black urologist, and Marion L. Poe, the first African American woman lawyer she knew, from her church, whom she admired and hoped to emulate, while crediting the bus incident with teaching her there was a difference because she was Black.

title: My First Time Away From Home
summary: George describes starting at Virginia Union University in fall 1959, the rules of the Baptist school requiring students to sign out, sneaking to Richmond on her own, and meeting Marie Sallason and Patricia Washington, roommates across the hall, with whom she got grounded by the dorm matron for laughing and carrying on.

title: She Could Pass for White
summary: She recounts sneaking down to basement dances and turning the clock back to beat curfew, driving to the Penn Relays in Philadelphia and staying at Patricia Washington's home, and how the light-skinned Patricia walked into a white barbershop in Richmond and got her hair cut while the others waited outside, a moment of resistance they found funny.

title: Two Ministers Pulled Us Together
topic: Hearing About the Sit-Ins and the Arrest
summary: George tells how news of the sit-ins spread from the Woolworth in North Carolina, how ministers Charles Sherrod and Frank Pinkston led the students in meetings and strategy in Richmond and prepared them for arrest and nonviolence, how she felt excited rather than afraid and never spoke of it, and how she came home, saw herself led out of the store on the night news, and knew she had to call her father.

title: The Richmond Room
summary: George describes marching downtown to the department store Thalhimers, where Black shoppers could spend money but had to eat at a basement stand and were barred from the segregated Richmond Room, which became the target because their money was good but their color shut them out, and recalls that they faced no sanctions and were greeted at churches with dinners while lawyers bailed them out.

title: A White Man Spit on Me
topic: The Arrest and Holding to Nonviolence
summary: George tells how the next step was buses into Mississippi until her father forbade it because he did not want her dead, recounts the arrest with her long fingernails making her hard to fingerprint and her lawyer arriving to bail her out, and describes a large white man spitting on her as she walked out after dark, when, separated from her friends, she was ready to do battle but stayed peaceful as required.

title: I Never Went In That Room
topic: Teaching, Her Father, and the Rules
summary: George explains that she later worked at Thalhimers over Christmas selling books but never entered the Richmond Room because access was what mattered, that after graduating she taught history about three years at Maggie L. Walker High School near Virginia Union, that her father told her on the phone only that as long as she was okay it was fine, and that the students accepted Virginia Union's restrictions and found ways around them without protesting.

title: We Didn't Think About Gender
summary: George says the students never thought about gender, grade or age, that she was eighteen or just nineteen, and that they only wanted to desegregate Richmond, ride at the front of the bus, and let Patricia Washington get her hair cut anywhere; she frames the February 1960 action as one incident and recalls Ella Baker's April meeting of student leaders at Shaw University, which Charles Sherrod attended.

title: Off the Welfare Rolls
summary: George describes earning a master's at the University of Maryland to become a guidance counselor, then working in the late 1960s for a private company on a federal contract in inner-city Washington, counseling Black people to get off welfare, off drugs and out of jail and helping single mothers in housing, and ties the work to the movement as helping people gain their rights and become viable citizens.

title: I Like to Give Back
summary: She recounts mentoring and resume counseling at Alfred University, where she was appointed to the Leadership Advisory Board and named an Honorary Alumna, the renaming of the Women's Leadership Center as the Judson Leadership Center after a sponsor died in an accident, and the training she continues to do there.

title: Credit for Desegregating Richmond
topic: The Fiftieth Anniversary and Her Silence
summary: George explains that she never talked about the sit-in until the fiftieth anniversary two years earlier, when, urged to attend, she went back with her niece and nephew and found a plaque bearing her name and learned the group was credited with desegregating Richmond, an effect she had never realized, and reflects that she does not brag, preferring to talk about her husband and son, while acknowledging her students might find it powerful to hear someone who acted at their age.

title: Six Facets of Service
summary: George describes Links, Incorporated, a national organization of predominantly African American women founded in Philadelphia, its six facets of service, and how her Springfield chapter mentors young girls and parents and supports a residence for women who have been incarcerated and on drugs, helping them overcome addiction, become better parents and find jobs and homes.

title: A Write-In Win
summary: She recounts the Links members who are lawyers, a judge and a UMass linguist, and her own civic roles in Northborough, Massachusetts: library trustee, chair of the Community Affairs Committee and the personnel board, and a reluctant write-in campaign her husband engineered that won her a seat on the Assabet Valley Regional Vocational Technical School committee.

title: He Is My Boss
summary: George explains her HR work and alternative dispute resolution that lets employees present grievances, and describes her husband Frederick's service as a selectman, board chair, and town moderator for ten years who facilitates the town meeting, appoints her to the personnel board, and reminds her that he is her boss, even calling the town administrator to ask how she had done her job.

title: We Both Keep Busy
summary: She describes how she and her husband both enjoy community work and support each other, his running the town's Applefest celebration with parades and fireworks, his human-capital consulting practice they can bounce ideas off, and why she would not want to be town moderator, lacking his patience and his ability to show no favoritism.

title: Just Keep Focused
summary: George rejects the image of a wholly nonviolent movement, saying it is hard not to be angry when spit on, recalls people yelling names as they marched while they avoided eye contact and stayed focused, and remembers the reunion where she reconnected with Marie Sallason and Patricia Washington and learned the group of thirty-four were called heroes for desegregating Richmond.

title: Racism Is Still Alive
summary: She shares that she believes racism is still rampant, that she was shocked to see an African American president elected and thought it was something her son would see, and that racism underlay the opposition Obama faced from senators and representatives who refused to let a brilliant man succeed.

title: He Doesn't See Color
topic: A Son in a Different World
summary: George contrasts her generation with her son, who never knew the back of the bus, whose friends are mostly white, who wanted his hair braided in seventh grade as the only African American in his class and now wears it large like Bob Marley without hiding his ethnicity, and who, unlike her, does not read setbacks as being about race.

title: Civil Rights in My Blood
summary: George says she has always had the civil rights fight in her blood and helps people who come to her at work, describing the subtle racism she sees, hiring a person of color or a woman but not giving them the full job or letting them advance, so the company loses them to another employer anyway.

title: Two Gender-Neutral Bathrooms
summary: She recounts taking on transgender issues at her manufacturing company of over four thousand people, pushing to win two gender-neutral bathrooms near the manufacturing floor, and rewriting training so managers and employees stop asking inappropriate questions about a person transitioning, framing it as understanding and valuing difference and letting people live their choices.

title: What's My Next Step
summary: George closes by wondering about her next step, whether to teach or do more community work, taking pride in winning the two bathrooms once the budget comes through and in completing the manager training, and the interview ends with thanks for her story as part of the Civil Rights History Project.
